posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked: 2019 Maison Leroy, Gamay. So I love me some Cru Beaujolais and a local shop had some of my usual suspects…but then I saw something out of the corner of my eye. ‘Leroy’. I’m not a big Burgundy guy but I’m obviously familiar with the producer. The conundrum was the price vs. Some of my other favorite producers. Was it work literally double the price?? Screw it, let’s give it a go! And while I fully expected to be a little disappointed…I’m definitely not! This is outstanding! Clean, pure gamay fruit but with a bit more complexity and structure compared to other Cru Beaujolais. Absolutley delicious and one of the more enjoyable bottles that I’ve had in a while. Is it work twice the cost of other top producers in the region?? I’m not sure…but I’m not mad that I bought this! Cheers!

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked: Great note! Glad you enjoyed it.

We had a bottle of this at Torc in Napa and thought it was excellent, but didn’t realize it was a shiner. Still delicious and currently cellaring some, maybe time to try another.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Ha! That isn’t how the Beaujolais works. Everything is for sale in bulk. The price of 100 liters of Chiroubles in bulk is around 220 EUR. A courtier would source the wine in bulk or in bottle, likely the former in the case of the Beaujolais, and then sell it to the négociant.

posts.display_username: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Jim Hanlon
posts.cooked: Probably not. This is a wine that would likely retail for less than $30 under its producer’s label. But folks are happy, even excited, to pay $65 or more because it has a Leroy label. This is a brilliant example of the power of brands and suggestion when tasting. That affects us all. And, to a certain extent, it’s fine. If folks feel like they got enough enjoyment out of the bottle for the price, then good for them. As others have said, this model has been pursued for decades.

posts.cooked: Went back and picked up the last two bottles that the store had. I guess they dont ‘produce’ much of this and the shop owner said its harder to get each year. Sounds like the 2020 should be released soon. Def going to try and jump on some when it is.

posts.name: Ry@n S m 1t h
posts.cooked:
From Bill Nanson:

“For me the Maison Bourgogne remains unclear if there is some blending involved on Leroy’s part or if it’s a purchase from a single producer - all the rest of the Maison wines are purchases, delivered in bottle, or occasionally in barrel - those bottles are re-corked with the Maison’s branded corks.

There is no wine-making involved for the Maison wines. Given the recorking and the necessity to add a bit more sulfur, then it’s not a big surprise that these wines come out a couple of years later than most other domaines’ wines…”
